notes.note: Pro tribunali: business done by a magistrate seated on the raised platform (tribunal) from which he gave judgement, as opposed to informal dealings; a mark of official, public action.

Provincial communities sent deputations (legationes) to Rome at public expense to thank or honour an outgoing governor before the Senate; Cicero curbed the cost of these laudatory embassies for Appius.

notes.anchor: the tax-farmers came to me at Samos
notes.note: The publicani, private companies who bid for the contract to collect provincial taxes; politically powerful at Rome, they pressed governors to favour their interests.

notes.anchor: the public council of the world, that is, in the Senate
notes.note: Cicero's grand formula (publicum orbis terrae consilium) for the Senate as the deliberative body of the whole Roman world, before which the embassies of thanks were to appear.

notes.anchor: the auctioning-off of the tributes and that most bitter exaction of the poll-tax and the door-tax
notes.note: Provincial taxes were sold off (venditio) to collectors; the capitatio (poll-tax) and a tax on house-doors (ostiarium) were among the harshest burdens on impoverished communities.
Conventus: the periodic court-sessions a governor held in the chief towns of his province to hear cases and administer justice on a fixed circuit.
Sententia bima: a motion to prolong Cicero's distasteful Cilician command to a second year; he begs Appius to have Hortensius drop it.
